I’ve recently been rewatching Marvel films, hoping to rekindle that spark—and Black Panther does exactly that. It’s packed with classic MCU charm, creativity, action, and an unforgettable soundtrack. For me, Black Panther stands as one of the strongest entries in the entire franchise. The soundtrack is phenomenal, easily the best Marvel has produced before or since. It also had the benefit of some characters already introduced in Civil War and Age of Ultron, the film hits the ground running with richer world-building than most solo debuts can achieve. The action is consistently strong, with the South Korea car chase remaining a goosebump-inducing highlight. The cast delivers across the board, but what really elevates the film is the dynamic between one of Marvel’s best heroes and best villains. Michael B. Jordan brings nuance and intensity to Killmonger, and I’d love to see the multiverse find a way to bring him back. And of course, Chadwick Boseman—an extraordinary actor who embodied T’Challa with grace, strength, and charisma—was the perfect Black Panther. Every time he appeared, he lit up the screen, and his absence will always be deeply felt. Not every element works flawlessly—the final fight is underwhelming, and I wish Boseman and Jordan had more scenes together to flesh out their dynamic—but the film does right outweighs its flaws. Rewatching it is both inspiring and bittersweet, but I’m grateful to revisit a film that left such a lasting mark.

Black kids needed a hero that looked like them. Everyone of all races of course love Spider-Man, Batman and Superman. But the parade of white superheroes just fueled the alienation of children of color because usually POCs were portrayed as bad guys and thugs. Not supervillians, just minions of supervillans. Then in 1966, Stan "The Man" Lee and Jack "The King" Kirby created T'Challa, the Black Panther and the king of the great African Kingdom, Wakanda. I never collected his solo title when I was a kid but he would pop up in other books I read and man, did he always look cool. As I got older, I started collecting his solo book and I was hooked. Comic books have a strong and loyal fanbase but most kids grow out of comics, but the big screen was another avenue to bring these modern day gods and legends to the masses. Ryan Coogler and Chadwick Boseman not only brought T'Challa to life in such a vibrant way, they made the Black Panther a household name. And this movie isn't just a great expansion of the character, having been introduced in 2016 Captain America: Civil War, it's an excellent movie all around! Great script, superb direction, strong cast, beautiful set design, music both score and soundtrack are excellent and on top of all that, flawless Visual and Special effects. This is in my TOP 5 of the MCU and Top 10 in Comic Book movies. Just the cast alone that includes Coogler's muse, Michael B. Jordan as the villainous Killmonger, a villain whom you understand why he does the things he does even if you can't condone his methods. Angela Bassett as the queen mother, Lupita Nyong'o as Nakia, a Wakandan spy and love interest for T'Challa. Then there's Danai Gurira as the fierce and loyal warrior, Okyoe and her husband W'Kabi played by Daniel Kaluuya of Get Out fame. Leticia Wright, Martin Freeman, Forest Whitaker, Sterling K. Brown, Andy Serkis and Winston Dukes stealing scenes as T'Challa's rival M'Baku. I mean, are you kidding me with this cast!? That is the power of a director like Ryan Coogler, he attracts talent like moths to a flame. His name belongs with Nolan and Tarantino as must see directors. He is an absolute genius! And he made a perfect comic book movie. Hell, he made a perfect movie!
